languages._comment_4x_skipped: '4.0' never existed as a real .NET Core/.NET release -- Microsoft deliberately skipped the entire 4.x number line when numbering .NET Core (jumping 3.1 -> 5.0) specifically to avoid confusion with the unrelated, much older .NET FRAMEWORK 4.x line (see '_comment_net_framework' below) that many developers were still using at the time. Listed here with include:false and a null release_date purely as an explicit 'this number was never used, not an oversight' reference row, matching this registry's convention of documenting gaps rather than silently omitting them.
languages._comment_net_framework: .NET FRAMEWORK (versions 1.0 through 4.8.1, released 2002-2022) is a COMPLETELY SEPARATE, Windows-only predecessor product line, not tracked anywhere in this registry -- not even as individual include:false rows, because its own version numbers (1.0, 1.1, 2.0, ...) would collide confusingly with .NET Core/.NET's identically-named-but-unrelated early versions in the SAME 'versions' array above. Out of scope for a structural reason, not a scope cut: .NET Framework only ships official Docker images under mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/framework/{sdk,aspnet}, and those images require WINDOWS CONTAINERS (Windows Server Core or Nano Server as the base OS) -- there has never been, and structurally cannot be, a Linux-container build of .NET Framework, since it depends on Windows-only APIs (full IIS/COM interop/registry access) baked into the runtime itself. This project's entire Docker pipeline (manager.py, dashboard.py, every other language's generator) assumes Linux containers throughout, so .NET Framework support would require an entirely separate Windows-container code path, not a registry entry -- a fundamentally different scope of work than anything else in this project, the same category of judgment call as this project declining to resurrect Java 6/7 via a proprietary Oracle JDK distribution.
languages._comment_11_preview: '11.0' is listed with include:false because it is STILL IN PREVIEW as of this writing (latest observed: 11.0.0-preview.5, published 2026-06-09) -- no GA/RTM release exists yet, so there is no stable SDK/aspnet Docker tag to build against. Re-flip to include:true once .NET 11 ships GA (expected ~November 2026 per Microsoft's established yearly November release cadence).
languages._comment_docker_repo: Verified live against the MCR v2 registry API (2026-07-07). Repo path moved twice: 'microsoft/dotnet' (old Docker Hub repo, 1.0-2.2 era -- now returns zero tags, dead) -> 'mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/core/{sdk,aspnet}' (2.0-3.1 era) -> unified 'mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet/{sdk,aspnet}' (2.1+, backfilled to 2.1). '2.2' and '3.0' were NEVER migrated to the unified repo -- their SDK/aspnet images exist ONLY under 'dotnet/core/sdk'/'dotnet/core/aspnet' (confirmed 404 on the new 'dotnet/sdk' path for both). See lang_dotnet.py's _sdk_repo()/_aspnet_repo() for the per-version dispatch this requires.
languages._comment_no_image: 1.0 and 2.0 are include:false because they have NO PULLABLE DOCKER IMAGE AT ALL, verified live against the MCR v2 tag-list API: 'dotnet/core/sdk:1.0' and 'dotnet/core/sdk:2.0' both 404 (as do their aspnet/runtime counterparts for 2.0; 1.0's bare core/runtime image technically still returns 200 but is useless without a buildable SDK). This mirrors the Java 6/7 finding (no live-or-archived Docker image exists for a language's own earliest tracked versions), except the .NET gap is smaller: EVERY version from 1.1 through 10.0 is genuinely pullable and buildable today, unlike Java's much larger 6/7 gap.
languages._comment_no_apt_needed: Unlike Go/Node/Java, NONE of the 5 crypto libraries tracked below need 'apt-get' for any system package: BouncyCastle.Cryptography is pure managed C#, NSec.Cryptography and LibOQS.NET both bundle their native binaries as NuGet package assets (pulled automatically by 'dotnet restore', no separate native-lib install step), and the two built-in System.Security.Cryptography entries need nothing beyond what the base image already ships. This means the recurring 'Debian/Ubuntu archive-mirror EOL' apt-get mitigation this project needed repeatedly for old Go/Node/Java base images (jessie/stretch/buster/bionic all now past their live-mirror window, per the OS-age research done for those languages) simply does not come up here -- 'dotnet restore'/'dotnet publish' never touches apt, so even a 1.1 (jessie-based) or 3.1 (buster-based, itself EOL since June 2024) build should succeed without any sources.list rewrite. Not build-tested end-to-end for every old version, but verified by design: no Dockerfile generated by lang_dotnet.py contains an apt-get line.

frameworks.name: ASP.NET Core
frameworks.notes: Always available -- versioned in lockstep with the .NET runtime itself (confirmed via Microsoft Learn: 'Microsoft.AspNetCore.App' became an implicit FrameworkReference baked into Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web starting 3.0, no separate NuGet version from then on; pre-3.0 it was a real metapackage with 'special versioning semantics handled outside of NuGet' but still tracked the runtime major.minor in practice). Two structurally different hosting-model templates are needed, not one (see _aspnetcore_program_tpl() in lang_dotnet.py): 1.1 through 5.0 use the classic split Startup.cs (ConfigureServices/Configure) + Program.cs calling WebHost.CreateDefaultBuilder(args).UseStartup<Startup>().Build().Run(), with routing done by hand via raw middleware (app.Run(context => ...) + manual context.Request.Path matching, since MVC routing attributes add unnecessary weight for a 2-endpoint app and the Minimal API syntax below did not exist yet). 6.0 onward use the unified top-level-statement Program.cs (var builder = WebApplication.CreateBuilder(args); ...) with genuine Minimal APIs (app.MapGet(...)) -- confirmed via the official 'Announcing ASP.NET Core in .NET 6' devblog post that Minimal APIs is an ASP.NET Core feature introduced in .NET 6, not a separate package.

frameworks.name: Carter
frameworks.notes: Thin routing library on top of ASP.NET Core's own endpoint routing (requires Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web + Kestrel -- every nuspec checked carries a FrameworkReference to Microsoft.AspNetCore.App). Verified live via the NuGet registration API: every major from 6.0.0 onward is SINGLE-TFM-PINNED with no multi-targeting (6.0.0 -> net6.0 only, 7.0.0 -> net7.0 only, etc.) -- Carter 7 will not build against a net8.0 SDK and vice versa, so compatibility here is an exact one-to-one match with the .NET version, not a range. Real API break at 5->6: pre-6.x (majors 3, 4, 5 -- '3.5.0' 2018-04-07 is Carter's earliest still-resolvable release on NuGet, '4.0.0' 2019-03-05, '5.0.0' 2019-11-25, all netstandard2.0-targeted, all kept as compatibility:[] REFERENCE-ONLY rows here since they predate .NET Core's endpoint routing model this project's app template relies on) used a Nancy-style abstract CarterModule base class with routes registered in the constructor; superseded entirely by 6.0.0's ICarterModule + void AddRoutes(IEndpointRouteBuilder app), which is the shape used unchanged through 10.0.0 and the only shape this project's lang_dotnet.py template implements. Still actively maintained (10.0.0 shipped 2025-11-13, matching .NET 10 GA).

frameworks.name: FastEndpoints
frameworks.notes: REPR-pattern (Request-Endpoint-Response) framework, needs ASP.NET Core/Kestrel underneath (FrameworkReference to Microsoft.AspNetCore.App confirmed in every nuspec checked). Verified live via the NuGet API: major '5' (2022-08-24) was a long-lived net6.0-only line that didn't roll to major 6 until 2025-04-13 -- almost 3 years on the same major, unusual for this project's other frameworks. From 6.0.0 onward net6.0 support was DROPPED entirely; 6/7/8 all multi-target net8.0/net9.0/net10.0 only. The core Endpoint<TRequest,TResponse> { Configure() / HandleAsync() } shape has been stable since roughly v2/v3 through the latest v8 -- one template covers majors 6-8 unchanged; only major 5 needs its own (older, still-compatible) template variant since it predates a config-registration API tweak (Verbs()/Routes() moved from callable-anywhere to enforced-only-inside Configure()). Majors '1' (2021-09-28, went through 6 beta/rc pre-releases first) through '4' (2022-03-30) predate that stabilization and are kept as compatibility:[] REFERENCE-ONLY rows -- real, NuGet-resolvable releases, just from FastEndpoints' own early churn period (roughly one major every 1-5 months during 2021-2022) before the API settled down for the long-lived v5 line this project actually tracks.

frameworks.name: NancyFx
frameworks.notes: Abandoned upstream -- GitHub repo archived (read-only) 2021-01-24, zero activity since; included specifically because the user asked for old-but-still-buildable coverage, the same rationale as this project's other deliberately-legacy buckets (BC bcprov-jdk15on '1.70', Conscrypt '1', Portable.BouncyCastle below). Verified live via NuGet: package id is plain 'Nancy' (NOT 'NancyFx.Nancy', which 404s); the last release published to NuGet is 2.0.0 (2019-04-27), targeting BOTH .NETFramework4.5.2 and .NETStandard2.0 -- the netstandard2.0 leg means it IS installable on modern net6.0-net10.0 SDKs, contrary to the assumption that Nancy is Framework-only. CORRECTION to an earlier, wrong claim in this registry: it previously stated 1.4.4 (which GitHub's own release notes call the 'final' 1.x release) was 'never republished to NuGet after 2.0.0' -- that was false, caught only when pulling Nancy's FULL NuGet version history for this reference-history pass: 1.4.4 (2018, exact date not the focus here) AND EVEN A NEWER 1.4.5 (2018-09-26) are both genuinely published on NuGet, postdating what GitHub calls 'final'. 2.0.0 remains what this registry actually tracks (it has the netstandard2.0 leg a Linux container needs, which is the deciding factor, not recency), but the '1' bucket below is kept as a real, resolvable REFERENCE-ONLY row (1.0.0, 2015-01-23, through 1.4.5) rather than a nonexistent one -- excluded from active tracking because 1.x uses Nancy's OLDER indexer-based routing syntax (Get["/"] = ...) instead of the method-call form (Get("/", ...)) this project's template implements for the tracked '2' bucket, a real template-shape difference, not a NuGet-availability gap. '0' bundles Nancy's entire pre-1.0 history (0.4.0 through 0.23.2, ~30 releases across 2011-2013) as a single REFERENCE-ONLY row -- too many individually-unremarkable early releases to enumerate one by one, dated to the earliest ('0.4.0', 2011-03-10). Nancy predates ASP.NET Core entirely: routes are registered in a NancyModule subclass's constructor using the method-call form Get("/", args => ...) (confirmed against Nancy's own master-branch sample module source -- NOT the older Get["/"] = ... indexer syntax some 1.x-era docs show), with its own Bootstrapper/self-hosting story (Nancy.Hosting.Self over raw System.Net.HttpListener) that this project deliberately does NOT use. HOSTING CHOICE: raw HttpListener self-hosting has a history of flaky/incomplete support on Linux .NET Core, so this registry instead hosts Nancy ON TOP of ASP.NET Core's own Kestrel via the standard OWIN bridge (Microsoft.AspNetCore.Owin + Nancy.Owin, app.UseOwin(x => x.UseNancy())) -- a well-established pattern for running legacy OWIN middleware under modern ASP.NET Core, and it lets this combo reuse the exact same Dockerfile/port setup as every other framework in this matrix instead of a bespoke HttpListener path. NuGet restore emits (harmless) NU1701 compatibility warnings for 'Nancy.Owin'/'Owin' being resolved via the .NETFramework fallback path rather than a native net6.0+ asset -- expected or note this as a real fact next time it's investigated, not a build failure. REAL RUNTIME BUG found via an actual docker run on EVERY request (not caught by a build-only check): 'System.InvalidOperationException: Synchronous operations are disallowed. Call WriteAsync or set AllowSynchronousIO to true instead.' -- Nancy.Responses.TextResponse (what the `Response res = someString;` implicit conversion produces) writes its body via a synchronous Stream.Write call baked into its pre-async/await Response model, and Kestrel disallows synchronous response-stream I/O by default since ASP.NET Core 3.0. There is no way to make TextResponse itself write asynchronously (Nancy has had no async story since the OWIN era) -- fixed with the standard, well-known remedy for hosting legacy sync-writing OWIN middleware under Kestrel: `builder.WebHost.ConfigureKestrel(o => o.AllowSynchronousIO = true)` before `builder.Build()`. Build-and-run verified end-to-end (both endpoints curled successfully) on .NET 8.0 after this fix.

frameworks.name: ServiceStack
frameworks.notes: Verified live via the NuGet API that ServiceStack's TFM groups are ADDITIVE across majors, not a rolling window: 5.0.0 ships net472+netstandard2.0; 6.0.0 adds net6.0 alongside net472; 8.0.0 adds net8.0 while KEEPING net6.0 and net472; 10.0.0 adds net10.0 while keeping net6.0/net8.0/net472. Every tracked major therefore restores cleanly against any SDK from net6.0 through net10.0 (NuGet picks the nearest-compatible asset group) -- confirmed no v7 or v9 was ever published on nuget.org, versioning deliberately tracks .NET's even-numbered LTS releases (6, 8, 10) with 5.0.0 as the original cross-platform/.NET-Core-capable baseline. The load-bearing gotcha, verified against ServiceStack's own current licensing FAQ: no license key is needed to build/start/run, but the FREE TIER CAPS OUT AT 10 REQUEST DTOs (Operations) -- a demo exceeding that throws a licensing exception at startup. Not a concern for this project (only 2 routes, '/' and '/version', are ever defined), but worth remembering if this app template is ever extended. Runs on top of ASP.NET Core/Kestrel in every tracked major (Microsoft.AspNetCore.App FrameworkReference present in every net6.0+ TFM group checked) via AppHostBase; the core Service subclass + [Route("/path")]-attributed request DTO pattern is unchanged across 5/6/8/10. REAL RUNTIME BUG, found via a live docker run crash on startup (a build-only check never caught it): `[Route("/")]` throws `System.NotSupportedException: RestPath '/' on Type 'RootRequest' is not Valid` -- root-caused by reading ServiceStack's own source (RestPath.cs): a route path is split into segments with empty entries removed, and the path is only considered valid once at least one non-empty segment exists; a bare '/' always splits into ZERO segments, so IsValid is unconditionally false for it regardless of any other attribute configuration. There is no [Route]-attribute workaround for the literal site root. Fixed by registering '/' as a plain ASP.NET Core Minimal API endpoint directly on `app` (before `app.UseServiceStack(...)`, so ordinary endpoint routing matches it first) and letting ServiceStack's [Route]-attributed request DTOs handle only '/version' -- confirmed both endpoints work correctly together in the same app via a live curl. Any future route added to this ServiceStack app template must avoid the bare '/' path the same way. Majors '2' (earliest resolvable release 2.2.2), '3' (earliest 3.0.0), and '4' (earliest 4.0.2) predate 5.0.0 and are kept as compatibility:[] REFERENCE-ONLY rows -- confirmed via NuGet these version numbers genuinely exist and were once installable, but NuGet's registration API reports a placeholder '1900-01-01' publish date for all three (a known artifact of packages uploaded before NuGet tracked real timestamps), so exact release dates are recorded here as null rather than a fabricated precise date; ServiceStack's own project history places 2.x around 2010-2011, 3.x around 2012-2013, and 4.x (the first commercially-licensed major) around 2014-2015. All three predate 5.0.0's cross-platform/.NET-Core capability -- they targeted Windows/.NET Framework/Mono only, structurally unbuildable in this project's Linux-container pipeline regardless of any template work, the same category of exclusion as this project's .NET Framework note at the language level.

cryptography_libs.name: System.Security.Cryptography
cryptography_libs.notes: The .NET BCL's own classical-crypto namespace (System.Security.Cryptography.*) -- part of every tracked runtime since .NET Core 1.1, no NuGet package needed. Touch/exercise line uses SHA256.Create(), available unchanged across the entire 1.1-10.0 range. Runtime-reported version is the .NET runtime's own version string (RuntimeInformation.FrameworkDescription), since this ships inbox with no independent version number of its own -- same reasoning Java applied to JCA.

cryptography_libs.name: System.Security.Cryptography.PQC
cryptography_libs.notes: Tracked as a SEPARATE built-in library entry from classical System.Security.Cryptography, mirroring how this project's Go registry splits 'crypto' from 'crypto/mlkem' (a built-in gated to Go >=1.24) -- same pattern, different language. Verified via the dotnet/runtime GitHub API-review issue (#114453, milestone 10.0.0, api-approved) and Microsoft's own .NET 10 announcement: the MLKem (FIPS 203 / ML-KEM), MLDsa (FIPS 204 / ML-DSA), SlhDsa (FIPS 205 / SLH-DSA), and CompositeMLDsa classes landed inbox in System.Security.Cryptography.dll starting .NET 10 (GA 2025-11-11) -- NOT in .NET 9 GA (PQC types were only reachable pre-10.0 via preview builds of the Microsoft.Bcl.Cryptography polyfill package layered on the .NET 9/10 dev cycle, never an inbox .NET 9 GA feature). In .NET 10, ML-KEM and ML-DSA had their [Experimental] gate REMOVED (fully supported); SLH-DSA and Composite ML-DSA remain marked [Experimental("SYSLIB5006")] due to limited OS support as of this writing. PLATFORM LIMITATION, not yet independently container-verified: these APIs are backed by MLKemOpenSsl/MLDsaOpenSsl on Linux, requiring OpenSSL 3.5+ at the OS level (Windows uses CNG instead) -- .NET 10's own default Linux base moved to Ubuntu 'noble' (24.04), which ships OpenSSL 3.0.13, NOT 3.5+, and this registry does not add any apt-get step to upgrade it (see the language-level '_comment_no_apt_needed' note). Concretely, this means MLKem.IsSupported / MLDsa.IsSupported will most likely evaluate to FALSE inside this project's own generated .NET 10 container until the base image itself ships OpenSSL 3.5+. The touch/exercise code checks IsSupported defensively and does not throw either way (mirrors Java Tink's try/catch-wrapped AeadConfig.register() -- 'exercised, ignore init failure') so the build and container startup still succeed regardless; only the runtime *availability* of PQC is affected, not buildability. Re-check this note once an mcr.microsoft.com/dotnet base image ships OpenSSL 3.5+ by default.

cryptography_libs.name: BouncyCastle.Cryptography
cryptography_libs.notes: Expanded from an earlier 4-bucket pinned-milestone design to EVERY real 2.x minor release (2.0-2.6, all dates confirmed via the NuGet registration API's 'published' field, superseding an earlier secondary-sourced 2024-12-04 date for 2.5 with the authoritative 2024-12-01) -- the user asked for major-version coverage across the board, and BC's own minor bumps are meaningful version boundaries (each ships real algorithm/API changes), not incidental patches, so collapsing them into 3-4 milestones was undercounting real history. The old 'rolling latest' bucket (previously plain '2') was DROPPED now that every minor is individually tracked -- it was purely redundant with whichever minor happens to be newest at generation time, and Go/Python's denser registries don't use rolling-latest placeholders either, preferring literal version numbers that get updated as new releases ship. See _bc_package() in lang_dotnet.py for the per-bucket package-id switch (the direct C# analog of Java's bcprov-jdk15on/jdk18on split): '1.9' is the LAST version of the old community-maintained 'Portable.BouncyCastle' package (1.9.0, 2021-10-19) -- still resolvable on nuget.org today, not marked deprecated on its NuGet page but effectively frozen/superseded once the official bcgit project began publishing directly. '2.0' (2022-11-15) is the OFFICIAL bcgit-published package's first release -- already included DRAFT post-quantum algorithms (Kyber, Dilithium, Falcon, SPHINCS+, and others), all explicitly flagged EXPERIMENTAL. PQC support for '2.1'/'2.2'/'2.3'/'2.4' is INFERRED as inheriting 2.0's draft status (not independently jar/dll-diffed minor-by-minor the way the 1.72/1.79/2.0/2.5 milestones were) -- defensible given the confirmed timeline (draft introduced 2.0, replaced wholesale at 2.5) but flagged as a slightly lower-confidence claim than the milestone versions themselves. '2.5' (2024-12-01) added the FINAL NIST-standardized ML-KEM/ML-DSA/SLH-DSA (FIPS 203/204/205) -- and, unlike bc-java (which kept its old draft Kyber/Dilithium names side-by-side for years after 1.79), bc-csharp REMOVED draft Kyber and SIKE support entirely in the same release, a sharper backward-compatibility break than the Java equivalent. '2.6' (2025-05-15, currently the latest STABLE minor at 2.6.2) inherits 2.5's final-name support. '2.7' is kept as an available:false REFERENCE row (compatibility left populated for when it does ship) because as of this writing it is still in beta only (2.7.0-beta.98, the latest of a long beta series) -- re-flip to available:true once a real 2.7.0 stable release lands. Note bc-csharp's PQC-milestone version numbers (2.0/2.5) are NOT aligned with bc-java's (1.72/1.79) even though both are the same underlying project family; don't assume the two languages' buckets correspond to the same calendar date.

cryptography_libs.name: NSec.Cryptography
cryptography_libs.notes: libsodium/NaCl wrapper, calendar-year-based versioning rather than this project's usual semantic-major convention -- each registry bucket resolves via the same startswith('{year}.')-prefix mechanism as every other library here, it just happens to look like a year. Expanded from a single rolling-latest bucket to every year that actually shipped a STABLE release (2018, 2019, 2020, 2022, 2024, 2025, 2026) -- verified live via the NuGet flatcontainer API that years 2021 and 2023 NEVER got a stable release at all, only preview-suffixed ones (21.11.0-preview2 and 23.5.0/23.6.0/23.9.0-preview.x respectively). Both are kept as explicit available:false REFERENCE rows ('21' dated to its one and only preview, 2021-11-13; '23' has no exact date recorded -- its per-version registration-API blob wasn't retrievable, only the flatcontainer version list confirming it exists) rather than silently omitted, so the gap in the version timeline reads as a verified fact, not an oversight in this registry. Compatibility per bucket is derived from each exact version's own nuspec TFM groups (checked individually, not assumed to be additive or backward-compatible across years the way ServiceStack's are): '18' targets netstandard1.1 (broadly compatible back to this project's earliest tracked 1.1), '19'/'20' target netstandard2.0(+2.1 for '20'), '22' adds a net5.0 group alongside netstandard2.1; netstandard2.1 itself floors at 3.0, but '22' pins its netstandard2.1 dependency group to EXACTLY 'System.Runtime.CompilerServices.Unsafe' 6.0.0 (no range, confirmed via the raw nuspec), and that specific transitive package version carries its own build-time TFM guard rejecting netcoreapp3.0 outright ('System.Runtime.CompilerServices.Unsafe doesn't support netcoreapp3.0. Consider updating your TargetFramework to netcoreapp3.1 or later.') -- found via a real failing `dotnet publish` on 3.0, NOT visible from '22's own nuspec TFM groups alone (which claim 3.0 compatibility) since the restriction lives one dependency hop away. Re-tested 3.1 with a real docker build immediately after and confirmed it publishes cleanly, so '22's real floor is 3.1, one full minor higher than its own netstandard2.1 targeting would suggest -- compatibility trimmed accordingly (3.0 dropped from the bucket). '24' targets net6.0 only (no netstandard fallback -- floor jumps to 6.0), '25' targets net8.0 only, '26' (current) targets net9.0 only. Verified live via NuGet: every version declares a hard PackageReference dependency on the separate 'libsodium' NuGet package, which ships prebuilt native binaries per-RID -- 'dotnet restore'/'publish' pulls the native libsodium binary automatically, no apt-get/system package needed (see the language-level '_comment_no_apt_needed' note). Only the current-era ('26') bucket has been independently container-verified with a real docker run; the older buckets' compatibility ranges are research-based from nuspec inspection, not yet build-tested end-to-end -- flagged the same way this project flags other research-based-but-untested version ranges. NSec has no PQC-relevant milestone at any version -- it is CLASSICAL-ONLY (Ed25519, X25519/ECDH, AES-GCM, ChaCha20-Poly1305, Blake2b; confirmed no ML-KEM/ML-DSA/SLH-DSA or any post-quantum primitive in the project's README or NuGet description across every tracked release).

cryptography_libs.name: LibOQS.NET
cryptography_libs.notes: The Node.js-equivalent-of-@noble/post-quantum / Go-equivalent-of-liboqs-go slot for .NET: a dedicated Open Quantum Safe (liboqs) binding exposing the FULL liboqs algorithm surface (ML-KEM/Kyber, ML-DSA/Dilithium, SLH-DSA/SPHINCS+, Falcon, etc.), directly relevant to this project's PQC mission in a way BouncyCastle/NSec/the built-in BCL aren't (liboqs tracks the reference/experimental algorithm set most closely). IMPORTANT DISTINCTION verified live via NuGet, easy to get wrong: the OFFICIAL open-quantum-safe/liboqs-dotnet project (0.1.0 Apr-2019 -> 0.2.0 Oct-2019 -> 0.3.0 Jul-2020) was NEVER published to nuget.org at all and its GitHub repo was archived/discontinued 2025-01-06 with an explicit 'not recommended, may have security vulnerabilities' notice -- it is NOT what this registry tracks. What IS tracked is an unrelated, independently-versioned COMMUNITY package also confusingly named 'LibOQS.NET' (id exactly 'LibOQS.NET', from github.com/filipw/maybe-liboqs-dotnet) with its OWN 0.1.0/0.2.0/0.3.0 release history (2025-09-10, 2026-01-21, 2026-01-23 respectively, all confirmed via the NuGet registration API's 'published' field) -- coincidentally similarly-numbered but six years later and completely unrelated to the official project's same-numbered releases. Expanded from tracking only the latest release (0.3) to all three real published minors, since the user asked for major-version coverage across the board -- checked each version's own nuspec directly rather than assuming they're all interchangeable: '0.1' targets ONLY net9.0 (no netstandard2.0 fallback group at all), so it is NOT installable on 6.0/7.0/8.0 -- compatibility is narrower than '0.2'/'0.3' for this reason, not an oversight. '0.2' added the netstandard2.0 fallback group (matching '0.3'), restoring broad 6.0-10.0 compatibility. The community package depends on a companion 'LibOQS.NET.Native' package (auto-pulled transitively, version-matched to the main package). Only STABLE releases are actively tracked; '0.4' (2026-07-01) is kept as an explicit available:false REFERENCE row -- it is a prerelease (0.4.0-rc1), filtered out of active resolution by the same stable-only version regex used for every other library in this project, but recorded here rather than silently omitted so it reads as a known, dated gap. Re-flip to available:true once a real 0.4.0 stable release lands. MATURITY CAVEAT, stated plainly rather than oversold: this is a young (~2K downloads at last check), pre-1.0, single-maintainer community package, not a mature production dependency -- included deliberately for PQC-research breadth (the project's stated mission favors breadth across the version timeline), not because it is battle-tested. TWO REAL RUNTIME BUGS found via an actual `docker run` on the '0.3' bucket (a live DllNotFoundException reported by a user, not caught in initial build-only verification) and both fixed, confirmed by re-running the container and curling both endpoints: (1) the nupkg DOES contain the real native binary per RID (extracted the .nupkg directly and confirmed `runtimes/linux-x64/native/liboqs.so` is present, 13MB) but a framework-dependent `dotnet publish` with no RuntimeIdentifier never copies RID-specific native assets into the publish output at all -- standard, documented .NET behavior, not a packaging defect -- fixed by setting `<RuntimeIdentifier>linux-x64</RuntimeIdentifier>` + `<SelfContained>false</SelfContained>` in app.csproj whenever this library is selected (see `_LINUX_RID` in `lang_dotnet.py`). (2) Even after the native file was correctly copied, `ldd` on it inside the container showed `GLIBC_2.34' not found` -- the prebuilt liboqs.so needs glibc >=2.34, which Debian bullseye (the default base for .NET 6.0/7.0 images) doesn't have (bullseye ships glibc 2.31); .NET 8.0/9.0 (bookworm, glibc 2.36+) and 10.0 (Ubuntu noble, glibc 2.39) are unaffected. Fixed by switching BOTH the sdk and aspnet image tags to the `-bookworm-slim` suffix specifically for the LibOQS.NET combo on 6.0/7.0 only -- every other framework/library pairing on 6.0/7.0 keeps the default bullseye-based tag (see `_image_tag_suffix()`/`_GLIBC_234_NEEDED_LIBS` in `lang_dotnet.py`). Build-and-run verified end-to-end (both endpoints curled successfully, liboqs actually initialized) on .NET 6.0, 7.0, 8.0, and 10.0 for the '0.3' bucket after these two fixes -- '0.1'/'0.2' inherit the same fixes automatically (both keyed off the library name, not the specific version bucket) but have not been independently re-verified with their own docker run.
